overview.body: In Module 1, you chose a destination from San Antonio — but never actually went anywhere! Time to hit the road. This unit, you'll copy your Lab 1.3 code, trim out the parts that no longer fit (the dice roll, the Galveston detour, the win/short logic), and add a while loop that simulates driving toward your destination. Each turn you travel a fixed 50 miles until you arrive. This is your first taste of the accumulator pattern — keeping a running total as the loop repeats.

mentorQuote.quote: The scariest bug in programming? An infinite loop. Before you write any while loop, I need you to answer one question: 'What makes this loop stop?' If you can't answer that clearly, you're not ready to code it yet. Test your exit condition first!

objectives: Trim your Lab 1.3 code: remove the dice roll, Galveston, and win/short logic
Simplify to 3 destinations (Corpus Christi, Houston, Austin)
Use a while loop to travel until you arrive
Display turn-by-turn progress and a trip summary when you arrive
overview.keyConceptsText: Chapter 4 Sections: 4.2 (The while Loop), 4.4 (Calculating a Running Total)
overview.textbookReference: Starting Out with Python, 6th Edition by Tony Gaddis — Chapter 4: Repetition Structures

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_CORPUS
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Corpus Christi (150 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_HOUSTON
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Houston (200 miles)

namedConstants.name: SAT_TO_AUSTIN
namedConstants.description: Distance from San Antonio to Austin (80 miles)

namedConstants.name: MILES_PER_TURN
namedConstants.description: Fixed travel distance per turn (50 miles) — NEW this unit!

programRequirements: Your program MUST start from your Lab 1.3 code (trim the dice roll, Galveston, and win/short logic)
Your program MUST use a while loop that continues while distance remaining is greater than 0
Your program MUST implement the accumulator pattern for tracking total miles
Your program MUST display a trip summary after arrival
Your program MUST work for all 3 destination choices

rows.task: Copy lab-1-3.py, trim the dice roll and Galveston logic, add MILES_PER_TURN
Verify clean state, add invalid-choice default branch
Set up tracking variables BEFORE the loop
Run end-to-end test, verify all 3 destinations, submit to CodeGrade

timeGuide.strategy: Build and test after EACH checkpoint! Don't write the entire program at once. Get each piece working before adding the next. This 'build a little, test a little' habit will save you hours of debugging.

beforeYouBegin.intro: Here's how your finished program fits together. The [TRASH] tags show what to delete from your Lab 1.3 code in CP1. The [NEW] tags show what you add this unit. Everything else is kept from Lab 1.3.
beforeYouBegin.architecture:
# ---- CONSTANTS ----
SAT_TO_CORPUS = 150           (keep from 1.3)
SAT_TO_HOUSTON = 200          (keep from 1.3)
SAT_TO_AUSTIN = 80            (keep from 1.3)
# HOU_TO_GALVESTON = 50       [TRASH-CP1]
MILES_PER_TURN = 50           [NEW-CP1]

# ---- MAIN PROGRAM ----
def main():
│
│── Welcome banner, player name      (keep from 1.3)
│── "You are in San Antonio" line    (keep from 1.3)
│
│── # "Roll to see how far..."      [TRASH-CP1]
│── # roll = int(input(...))         [TRASH-CP1]
│── # miles_rolled = roll * 10       [TRASH-CP1]
│
│── Display 3-choice menu             (keep from 1.3)
│── choice = int(input('...'))      (keep from 1.3)
│
│── if choice == 1:                  (keep, but TRIM body)
│       destination = 'Corpus Christi'
│       distance = SAT_TO_CORPUS
│── elif choice == 2:                (keep, but TRIM body)
│       destination = 'Houston'
│       distance = SAT_TO_HOUSTON
│── elif choice == 3:                (keep, but TRIM body)
│       destination = 'Austin'
│       distance = SAT_TO_AUSTIN
│── else:                            [NEW-CP1]
│       default to Corpus + warning
│
│── miles_remaining = distance    [NEW-CP2]
│── total_miles = 0                  [NEW-CP2]
│── turn = 1                         [NEW-CP2]
│
│── Print journey header             [NEW-CP3]
│
│   TRAVEL LOOP:                       [NEW-CP3/4]
│   while miles_remaining > 0:
│   │── Display turn header
│   │── Display miles remaining
│   │── Display travel message
│   │── miles_remaining -= MILES_PER_TURN
│   │── total_miles += MILES_PER_TURN
│   │── turn += 1
│
│── Arrival announcement             [NEW-CP5]
│── TRIP SUMMARY                     [NEW-CP6]
│       (different from 1.3: no Status,
│        adds Total Turns and Total Miles)
│── Outcome message                  [NEW-CP6]
│       (different from 1.3: based on
│        destination, not status)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

content.body: A trimmed-down version of your Lab 1.3 code, ready for the while-loop journey. After this checkpoint, your file will have the banner, name input, menu, and destination/distance logic from Lab 1.3 — minus the dice roll, the Galveston detour, and the win/short outcome logic that no longer fits this unit's mechanic.

Refactoring an existing file is how 90% of real software gets built. You're not throwing away working code — you're surgically removing what no longer fits and keeping what still works. The banner, name input, and menu pattern you wrote in Module 1 don't go anywhere. The dice roll and fall-short logic do, because we're modeling the trip as a journey instead of a single roll.
Setup:

items: Copy lab-1-3.py to lab-2-1.py (File → Save As, or your editor's equivalent)

Update the docstring header: change the lab title to Lab 2.1: Keep On Driving, update the description, and change the Chapter 3 Concepts section to Chapter 4 (while loop, Loop counter, Accumulator pattern)

content.body: TRASH (delete these from your code):

items: The HOU_TO_GALVESTON constant at the top of the file

The entire dice roll block: the "Roll to see how far you can travel." print statements, the URL print, the roll = int(input(...)) line, the miles_rolled = roll * 10 calculation, and the "You rolled X! That gives you Y miles." print
The initialize-defaults block before the if-elif-else (destination = 'Nowhere', distance = 0, miles_remaining = miles_rolled, status = 'ERROR', message = 'Something went wrong!')
Everything inside if choice == 1: EXCEPT the destination = 'Corpus Christi' line
Everything inside elif choice == 2: (Houston) EXCEPT the destination = 'Houston' line — this includes the entire nested Galveston logic
Everything inside elif choice == 3: (Austin) EXCEPT the destination = 'Austin' line
Everything inside the original else: branch (we rebuild this branch from scratch in CP2)
The entire OUTPUT SECTION: every line from the "Display heading" if-statement through the final print(f'Miles to spare: {miles_remaining}')
In the TRIP SUMMARY: the Miles Rolled:, Miles Remaining:, and Status: lines
The final if-elif-else that prints a closing message based on status

content.body: KEEP (don't touch these):

items: The three distance constants: SAT_TO_CORPUS, SAT_TO_HOUSTON, SAT_TO_AUSTIN
The main() structure and the if __name__ == '__main__' block
The welcome banner (the five print statements with equal signs)
The player name input and welcome message (player_name = input(...), print(f'Welcome, {player_name}!'), print('You are in San Antonio. Your goal: reach the beach!'))
The destination menu display (the five print statements showing the 3 choices)
The choice = int(input('Enter your choice (1-3): ')) line
The if choice == 1:, elif choice == 2:, elif choice == 3: structure — you're trimming their bodies, not deleting the branches themselves
The TRIP SUMMARY banner lines and the Driver:, Destination:, Distance: lines

content.body: RENAME (small but important):

items: Inside each branch, the variable that holds the distance to your destination was called distance in Lab 1.3. Rename it to distance throughout this file. You'll touch it in three places: the branches, and the Distance: line in the TRIP SUMMARY

content.body: ADD (just one thing for now):

items: Add the new constant under your distance constants: MILES_PER_TURN = 50
Add a comment above it: # Travel constant

content.title: What You're Keeping From Module 1
content.body: Every pattern you're keeping was introduced in a Module 1 BookEx and is still valid. The input() pattern (Program 2-13), the if-elif-else structure (Program 3-6), and f-string output (Program 2-20) all carry forward unchanged. You're not learning these again — you're reusing them.

content.quote: Patch Notes incoming. Look at the else branch you're about to add in CP2 — we're going to default invalid input to Corpus Christi and print a warning. That's not a real solution; that's a stopgap. T-Bone flagged the validation gap back in Lab 1.3, and we're still not fixing it this unit. In Lab 2.3 we'll write get_valid_int() and finally close this hole. Until then, the door is still wide open. Notice every place we're working around input we can't trust — that's where the real refactoring lives.

content.body: Run your program. After cleanup, it should still work — just truncated. You'll see the banner, the name prompt, the menu, the choice prompt, then "Heading to {destination}!" (which is leftover from Lab 1.3 and will get moved in CP2) and the trip summary header. The most important thing: no NameErrors from leftover Galveston or status references.

content.title: If you see NameError
content.body: NameError: name 'miles_rolled' is not defined means you missed a reference to the dice roll in some print statement. NameError: name 'status' is not defined means you missed a reference in the trip summary. Search your file for the broken name and delete the line that uses it.

rows: Deleting destination = 'Corpus Christi' by accident
Trip summary will show NameError — keep the destination assignment in each branch
Leaving a miles_remaining reference in a print
NameError as soon as the program reaches that line
Forgetting to rename distance → distance
Lab 2.1 uses distance consistently. Mixing names will cause CodeGrade tests to fail
Deleting the if-elif-else branches entirely instead of trimming their bodies
The structure stays the same — you keep the if choice == N: lines, you just trim what's inside each branch

items: A lab-2-1.py file that runs without errors
Three distance constants plus MILES_PER_TURN = 50
Welcome banner, name input, "You are in San Antonio" line
Destination menu and choice input (still using int(input()) from Module 1)
Three branches that set destination and distance only (no Galveston, no win/short, no status)
Trip summary banner and Driver/Destination/Distance lines
No NameErrors when you run the program

content.body: Confirmation that your cleanup from CP1 left you in a working state, plus the new else: branch that handles invalid menu input by defaulting to Corpus Christi.

Every working program needs a strategy for invalid input. Without an else: branch, choosing 4 or typing anything unexpected would leave your destination and distance variables undefined, crashing the while loop in CP4. The graceful-default pattern (assign valid values, print a warning) keeps the program running even when the user gives bad input.

items: Run your file from CP1 one more time. Choose 1, 2, and 3 in separate runs and verify each sets a different destination
Try choosing 4 — the program may crash with NameError because no branch matched. That's what we're about to fix
After the elif choice == 3: branch, add a new else: branch
Inside the else: branch, set destination = 'Corpus Christi' and distance = SAT_TO_CORPUS
Add a print line: print('Invalid choice. Defaulting to Corpus Christi.')

references.description: The if-elif-else pattern with a catch-all else branch. You used this exact shape in Lab 1.2 and Lab 1.3 — the new else here serves the same purpose.

rows: Hardcoding distances instead of using constants
CodeGrade checks for constants — use SAT_TO_CORPUS, not 150
Swapping destination and distance values
Houston is 200 miles, Corpus is 150 — test each choice carefully
Forgetting the warning print in the else branch
Player gets defaulted to Corpus silently, which is a worse user experience

items: All four branches (choices 1, 2, 3, and else) setting destination and distance
The else branch prints a warning before defaulting
Choosing 4 (or any invalid input) doesn't crash — it shows the warning and defaults to Corpus Christi

content.body: Three variables: one to track how far you still need to go, one to accumulate total miles traveled, and one to count turns.
The #1 accumulator mistake is initializing inside the loop. If you set total = 0 inside the loop, it resets to 0 every iteration! You must initialize BEFORE the loop.

references.description: You initialized total = 0.0 BEFORE the loop, then added to it inside. Same pattern here — initialize your tracking variables before the loop!

content.body: After your if-elif-else chain (where destination and distance are set), add three variables:

items: miles_remaining — starts equal to the full distance
total_miles — accumulator, starts at 0
turn — counter, starts at 1

content.body: Step 1: Add these debug lines AFTER your three variable initializations:

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 3
    print(f'DEBUG: miles_remaining={miles_remaining}')
    print(f'DEBUG: total_miles={total_miles}')
    print(f'DEBUG: turn={turn}')

content.body: Step 2: Run with choice 1 (Corpus Christi). Verify:

content.output:
DEBUG: miles_remaining=150
DEBUG: total_miles=0
DEBUG: turn=1

content.body: Step 3: Run again with choice 3 (Austin). Verify:

content.output:
DEBUG: miles_remaining=80
DEBUG: total_miles=0
DEBUG: turn=1

content.title: Delete all three debug lines
content.body: Delete all three debug lines before moving to Checkpoint 3.

rows: Initializing variables INSIDE the loop
They reset every iteration! Initialize BEFORE the loop starts
Starting turn at 0 instead of 1
Turn 1 is the first turn — human counting starts at 1!
Putting tracking variables before if-elif-else
You need to know distance first before setting miles_remaining

items: Three tracking variables initialized AFTER if-elif-else, BEFORE the loop
miles_remaining equals the distance for the chosen destination
total_miles is 0, turn is 1

content.body: A while loop that continues as long as there's still distance to travel. Think of it as: "Keep driving WHILE there are miles remaining."

The while condition is the most important decision in any loop. T-Bone said it in the overview: if you can't clearly answer "what makes this loop stop?" you're not ready to write it. Here the answer is concrete — miles_remaining decreases by MILES_PER_TURN every iteration until it hits zero or below. The condition guards against the loop running forever.

references.description: You wrote a loop that continued while temperature > MAX_TEMP. Your travel loop continues while miles_remaining > 0. Same pattern!

items: Add a "journey begins" header with print statements before the loop
Write the while statement: continue while miles_remaining is greater than 0
Don't forget the colon — while condition: requires it
For now, just add a placeholder inside the loop: print the turn number

content.title: Infinite Loop Danger!
content.body: If you run this now without updating miles_remaining inside the loop, it will run forever. Press Ctrl+C to stop it. Use the test harness below to safely test your loop structure.
Step 1: Add this test harness INSIDE your while loop (indented!):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 4
    print('DEBUG: Loop entered! Condition was True.')
    print(f'DEBUG: miles_remaining={miles_remaining}')
    break  # TEMPORARY - stops loop after 1 iteration

content.body: Step 2: Run with choice 1 (Corpus Christi). Verify:

content.output:
DEBUG: Loop entered! Condition was True.
DEBUG: miles_remaining=150

content.body: Step 3: This confirms your while condition is correct — the loop entered because miles_remaining (150) is greater than 0.

content.title: Delete all three debug/break lines
content.body: Delete all three debug/break lines before moving to Checkpoint 4.

rows: Using while miles_remaining != 0
You might overshoot 0! Use > 0 instead
Forgetting the colon after the while condition
SyntaxError — while condition: needs that colon!
Loop body not indented
Everything INSIDE the loop must be indented one level

items: A while miles_remaining > 0: loop in place
The loop body is empty for now (you'll add the body in CP5)
Or, if you wrote a placeholder body, verify it doesn't infinite-loop

content.body: The complete loop body that: shows turn info, travels 50 miles, updates miles_remaining so the loop eventually ends, accumulates total_miles, and increments the turn counter.

The accumulator pattern (total_miles = total_miles + MILES_PER_TURN) is one of the most common shapes in all of programming. Shopping carts add prices. Game scores tally points. Fitness apps count steps. Every running total in every program you'll ever use is some version of this exact line.
Inside your while loop (indented!), do these things in order:

items: Display the turn header — show what turn number this is
Display miles remaining — so the player sees their progress
Display a travel message — "You travel 50 miles..." (use your constant!)
Update miles_remaining — SUBTRACT miles traveled
Update total_miles — ADD miles traveled (accumulator pattern!)
Increment turn — add 1 so next iteration shows Turn 2, Turn 3, etc.

references.description: You wrote total = total + number to accumulate. Your version: total_miles = total_miles + MILES_PER_TURN

rows: Forgetting to subtract from miles_remaining
Infinite loop! Distance never reaches 0
Using = instead of = ... + for accumulator
Overwrites instead of accumulating — always shows 50
Wrong indentation
Code outside loop only runs once; code inside repeats
Forgetting to increment turn
Every turn displays as Turn 1

content.body: Step 1: Add this debug line at the END of your while loop (still indented inside the loop):

content.code:
# DEBUG - DELETE BEFORE CHECKPOINT 5
    print(f'DEBUG END OF TURN: remaining={miles_remaining}, total={total_miles}, next_turn={turn}')
    print()

content.body: Step 2: Run with choice 1 (Corpus Christi, 150 mi). Verify you see exactly 3 turns:

content.output:
--- Turn 1 ---
Miles remaining: 150
You travel 50 miles...
DEBUG END OF TURN: remaining=100, total=50, next_turn=2

--- Turn 2 ---
Miles remaining: 100
You travel 50 miles...
DEBUG END OF TURN: remaining=50, total=100, next_turn=3

--- Turn 3 ---
Miles remaining: 50
You travel 50 miles...
DEBUG END OF TURN: remaining=0, total=150, next_turn=4

content.body: Step 3: Verify these critical values: remaining decreases by 50 each turn (150 → 100 → 50 → 0), total increases by 50 each turn (50 → 100 → 150), next_turn increments (2 → 3 → 4), and the loop STOPS when remaining hits 0.

content.title: Delete the debug lines
content.body: Delete the debug lines before moving to Checkpoint 5.

items: Loop runs the correct number of turns for each destination (3 for Corpus, 4 for Houston, 2 for Austin)
Turn counter increments each iteration
Miles remaining decreases each turn
Loop stops when miles_remaining reaches 0

content.body: Code that runs after the while loop (fully unindented!) to announce arrival and display trip statistics.
Code INSIDE the loop repeats. Code AFTER the loop (unindented) runs once when the loop is done. This is where you celebrate arrival and show the summary.

content.quote: The Trip Summary is your user's reward for completing the journey! Make it feel like an achievement — use separator lines, clear labels, and organized data. A well-designed summary turns raw numbers into a story of their adventure.

content.body: After the while loop (no indentation!), add:

items: Arrival announcement — "You made it to {destination}!"
Trip Summary header with separator lines
Display Driver (player_name), Destination, Distance, Total turns, and Total miles
Display total turns as turn - 1 (see warning below)
Outcome message — different text for Corpus Christi (beach!) vs other destinations

content.title: Why turn - 1?
content.body: You increment turn at the END of each loop iteration. After 3 turns complete, turn equals 4 when the loop exits. So display turn - 1 for the actual count.

references.description: F-string output with embedded variables — same pattern from Module 1, now used for the Total Turns and Total Miles lines in your trip summary.

content.body: Complete output with name "Maria" and choice 1 (Corpus Christi):

==================================================

What is your name, traveler? Maria

  1. Corpus Christi - 150 miles (BEACH!)
  2. Houston - 200 miles
  3. Austin - 80 miles

Enter your choice (1-3): 1

Heading to Corpus Christi!

==================================================

Thanks for playing!

content.body: Also test all three destinations and verify turn counts: Choice 1 (Corpus Christi, 150 mi) → 3 turns, beach message. Choice 2 (Houston, 200 mi) → 4 turns, different message. Choice 3 (Austin, 80 mi) → 2 turns, no beach message.

Run your program through all three destinations. Corpus should take 3 turns (150 / 50 = 3). Houston should take 4 turns (200 / 50 = 4). Austin should take 2 turns (80 / 50 = 1.6 → 2). Compare against the Expected Output above. For the full submission check, see the Final Check & Submit section below.

rows: Arrival message INSIDE the loop
Prints every turn! Put it AFTER the loop ends
Using turn instead of turn - 1 for total turns
Turn increments at end of loop — after exiting, it's one too high
Wrong beach logic
Only Corpus Christi has a beach — check destination name exactly

items: Arrival message appears once (not every turn)
Trip Summary shows correct turn count and total miles
Different outcome messages for beach vs. non-beach destinations
Complete program works for all 3 destinations

beforeYouSubmit: Test ALL three destination choices and verify correct turn counts: Corpus Christi (150 mi) → 3 turns, Houston (200 mi) → 4 turns, Austin (80 mi) → 2 turns
Docstring includes name, abc123, section, and description

ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Cleanup) — you missed a reference to a Lab 1.3 variable when trimming. Search your file for the broken name and delete the line that uses it.
Check CP1 (Cleanup) — the new constant is missing from the top of the file. Add MILES_PER_TURN = 50 with your other constants.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Output skips the else: default and crashes with NameError: name 'destination' is not defined when choosing 4
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP2 (Setup & Simplify) — the else branch wasn't added. Every if-elif chain needs an else for invalid input.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Program runs forever (infinite loop) when you start the journey
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP4 (Build the while Loop) — your loop body isn't decreasing miles_remaining. The line miles_remaining = miles_remaining - MILES_PER_TURN needs to be INSIDE the loop.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Total Miles in the Trip Summary is 0 or the same as MILES_PER_TURN
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP3 (Initialize Tracking Variables) — you initialized total_miles = 0 inside the loop instead of before it. The initialization resets every iteration. Move it to BEFORE the while line.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Total Turns shows one more than expected (e.g., 5 for Houston instead of 4)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP5 (Complete the Loop Body) — you're displaying turn after incrementing it. The fix is to use turn - 1 in the trip summary, which the solution does intentionally.

ifSomethingBreaks.symptom: Trip Summary still shows the lines from Lab 1.3 (Miles Rolled, Miles Remaining, Status)
ifSomethingBreaks.redirect: Check CP1 (Cleanup) — these three lines should have been trashed. Search the file for Miles Rolled and Status: and delete those print lines.

ifYouGetStuck: Re-read Program 4-2 (temperature.py) and Program 4-17 (sum_numbers.py) — the while loop and accumulator patterns are exactly what you need
Add DEBUG prints before and inside the loop to see what's happening
Check your indentation — everything inside the loop needs 4 spaces
Submit to CodeGrade early — it shows exactly which tests pass or fail
Come to Zoom or office hours — we can screen share and debug together
needHelp.bookexResourceNote: Chapter 4 BookEx Reference Guide — step-by-step breakdowns of every textbook example, your primary reference
